questions.question: Define Statistical Inference and explain its importance in modern data-driven applications.
keyPoints: Statistical Inference is drawing conclusions about a population from sample data
Enables making valid predictions without examining entire population
Essential for big data applications where examining all data is impractical
Provides mathematical framework for decision-making

questions.question: Distinguish between Population and Sample. Also explain the relationship between Parameter and Statistic.
keyPoints: Population is entire set of observations; Sample is subset
Parameter is population characteristic (μ, σ)
Statistic is sample characteristic (x̄, s)
Statistics are used to estimate parameters

questions.question: Explain the difference between Descriptive Statistics and Inferential Statistics with examples.
keyPoints: Descriptive statistics summarizes data (mean, median, mode)
Inferential statistics uses sample to predict about population
Descriptive: organizing and presenting data
Inferential: making predictions and testing hypotheses

questions.question: What is a Random Sample? Why is it important in statistical inference?
keyPoints: Random sample gives every population member equal chance of selection
Reduces selection bias
Ensures representativeness of sample
Foundation for valid statistical inference

questions.question: Explain Point Estimation and Interval Estimation with an example.
keyPoints: Point estimation provides single value estimate (e.g., sample mean as estimate of population mean)
Interval estimation provides range (e.g., confidence interval)
Point estimate may not capture uncertainty
Interval provides level of confidence about parameter location

questions.question: What is Sampling Error? Can it be eliminated completely?
keyPoints: Sampling error is difference between sample statistic and true population parameter
Occurs naturally due to random sampling
Cannot be eliminated completely but can be reduced by larger samples
Different from bias which is systematic error

questions.question: Describe the two main branches of Statistical Inference.
keyPoints: Estimation: Point estimation and Interval estimation
Hypothesis Testing: Testing claims about population parameters
Estimation provides values for unknown parameters
Hypothesis testing evaluates claims/relationships

questions.question: Explain the concept of Statistical Inference in detail. Discuss the need for sampling in statistical inference, and describe the characteristics of a good sample.
keyPoints: Statistical Inference: process of drawing conclusions about population from sample data
Need for sampling: practical impossibility of examining entire populations especially with big data
Characteristics of good sample: random selection, representative, adequate size, minimizes bias
Types: random sampling, stratified sampling, systematic sampling
Role of sample size in inference accuracy

questions.question: Define Population, Sample, Parameter, and Statistic. Explain with an example how sample statistics are used to estimate population parameters.
keyPoints: Population: complete set of all observations
Sample: subset of population selected for study
Parameter: numerical characteristic of population (μ, σ)
Statistic: numerical characteristic of sample (x̄, s)
Example: Measuring heights of 100 students to estimate average height of all 10,000 students
x̄ used as point estimate for μ, confidence interval provides range

questions.question: What is the Central Limit Theorem? Explain its importance in statistical inference with suitable example.
keyPoints: CLT: Sampling distribution of mean approaches normal distribution as sample size increases
Works regardless of population distribution shape
Enables use of normal-based inference methods
Example: Rolling dice many times, distribution of sample means becomes bell-shaped
Foundation for confidence intervals and hypothesis testing

questions.question: Discuss the concept of Hypothesis Testing in statistical inference. Explain the roles of Null Hypothesis and Alternative Hypothesis.
keyPoints: Hypothesis testing: method to test claims about population using sample data
Null Hypothesis (H0): default assumption of no effect/difference
Alternative Hypothesis (H1): claim we want to test
Test statistic calculated from sample data
Decision: reject H0 if evidence is strong, otherwise fail to reject
Types of errors: Type I (false positive), Type II (false negative)
